foods.name: Honey
foods.narration: Imam al-Ṣādiq (ʿa) pointed to honey as a foremost remedy, echoing the Qur'an's words that in it 'is healing for people.' The Imams encouraged it as a cure taken with intention.
foods.sunnahTip: Take a little honey on an empty stomach in the morning.
foods.nutritionNote: Natural sugars for quick energy, with antioxidant and antibacterial compounds.

foods.name: Dates
foods.narration: The Ahlul Bayt loved dates and especially praised the ʿajwa date of Madīnah. Breaking the fast with dates is reported as their consistent practice.
foods.sunnahTip: Break your fast with an odd number of fresh dates, then pray.
foods.nutritionNote: Natural sugars, fiber, potassium and iron — steady energy without a crash.

foods.name: Olive & Olive Oil
foods.narration: Of the olive the Imams said: eat the oil and anoint yourselves with it, for it comes from a blessed tree — reflecting the Qur'an's 'a blessed olive tree.'
foods.sunnahTip: Use olive oil in food and rub a little on skin and hair.
foods.nutritionNote: Heart-friendly monounsaturated fats and vitamin E.

foods.name: Figs
foods.narration: Allah swears by the fig in the Qur'an. Narrations of the Ahlul Bayt praise it for easing the stomach and strengthening the body and bones.
foods.sunnahTip: Eat a few figs; they were said to be closest to a fruit of Paradise.
foods.nutritionNote: Fiber, potassium and calcium that aid digestion and bones.

foods.name: Pomegranate
foods.narration: Imam al-Bāqir and Imam al-Ṣādiq (ʿa) encouraged eating pomegranate, with its soft pith, reporting that it cleanses the stomach and brightens the heart.
foods.sunnahTip: Eat pomegranate on an empty stomach, including the soft inner pith.
foods.nutritionNote: Rich in vitamin C and antioxidants (polyphenols) that support the heart.

foods.name: Grapes & Raisins
foods.narration: The Ahlul Bayt recommended raisins, reported to strengthen the body, settle the temperament and clear phlegm.
foods.sunnahTip: Eat a small handful of raisins, especially the red ones.
foods.nutritionNote: Concentrated natural sugars, iron and antioxidants — quick energy.

foods.name: Milk
foods.narration: The Qur'an calls it 'pure milk, pleasant.' The Imams encouraged drinking it, noting it strengthens the back, and taught the supplication for increase in it.
foods.sunnahTip: Drink fresh milk; it was a favored drink of the Prophet (ṣ).
foods.nutritionNote: Protein, calcium and vitamin D for bones and muscles.

foods.name: Fish
foods.narration: The Qur'an calls the catch of the sea 'fresh meat.' The Imams ate and permitted fish that has scales, while counseling moderation.
foods.sunnahTip: Choose fish that has scales; eat it in moderation.
foods.nutritionNote: Lean protein and omega-3 fats that support the heart and brain.

foods.name: Ginger
foods.narration: Ginger (zanjabīl) is named among the drinks of Paradise. Narrations mention it sharpens the mind and aids digestion.
foods.sunnahTip: Take a little ginger to warm the stomach and aid digestion.
foods.nutritionNote: Eases nausea and supports digestion; warming and anti-inflammatory.

foods.name: Gourd & Squash
foods.narration: Gourd (yaqṭīn / dubbāʾ) grew over the Prophet Yūnus (ʿa). The Prophet Muḥammad (ṣ) is reported to have loved it, and narration relates that it strengthens the heart and mind.
foods.sunnahTip: Add gourd or squash to your soups and stews.
foods.nutritionNote: Hydrating, light and rich in vitamin A; gentle on digestion.

foods.name: Lentils
foods.narration: Lentils (ʿadas) are reported as blessed and softening to the heart, bringing tears of humility; the Ahlul Bayt encouraged them.
foods.sunnahTip: Include lentils in your meals; they were called a food of the righteous.
foods.nutritionNote: Plant protein, fiber and iron — filling and steadying.

foods.name: Cucumber
foods.narration: Among the foods named in the Qur'an (2:61). The Prophet (ṣ) is reported to have eaten cucumber together with dates, balancing its coolness.
foods.sunnahTip: Eat cucumber with a little date or honey to balance its coolness.
foods.nutritionNote: Very hydrating and cooling, with light fiber and minerals.

foods.name: Basil & Fragrant Herbs
foods.narration: Fragrant herbs (rayḥān) are named among Allah's bounties. The Imams loved good scent and encouraged sweet basil, disliking the refusal of a gift of fragrance.
foods.sunnahTip: Keep and smell sweet basil; do not refuse a gift of fragrance.
foods.nutritionNote: Aromatic herbs aid appetite and digestion and lift the mood.

foods.name: Water
foods.narration: From water Allah made every living thing. The Imams taught the manners of drinking — in three breaths, beginning with His name and ending with praise.
foods.sunnahTip: Sip water in three breaths; say Bismillāh before and al-ḥamdu lillāh after.
foods.nutritionNote: The basis of life and every bodily function — stay well-hydrated.
